title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents a novel approach to object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds, introducing a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker that achieves state-of-the-art performance. The method is well-motivated, and the technical details are clearly described.

**Strengths:**

* The paper is well-organized, and the writing is clear and concise.
* The proposed method is technically sound, and the motivation behind the design choices is well-explained.
* The experimental results are impressive, showing significant improvements over previous methods.
* The related work is adequately discussed, and the paper provides a clear understanding of the contributions.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions:**

While the paper demonstrates promising results, there are some areas that require further clarification and justification. Specifically:

* Some claims regarding the method's performance, particularly in sparse point clouds, are not fully supported by experimental evidence. Additional experiments or analyses would strengthen these claims.
* The ablation study only considers the car category, which may not be representative of the method's performance on other categories. Conducting ablation studies on all four categories would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the method's contributions.
* Some statements, such as the relationship between stability and accuracy, require further clarification to ensure a clear understanding of the method's limitations.

**Recommendations:**

To address these concerns, I recommend the following:

* Provide additional experimental evidence to support the claims regarding the method's performance in sparse point clouds.
* Conduct ablation studies on all four categories to demonstrate the method's effectiveness across different scenarios.
* Clarify the limitations of the proposed method, particularly with regards to its suitability for indoor scenes.

**Overall Assessment:**

Despite these limitations, the paper presents a novel and effective approach to object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The proposed method demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, and the technical details are well-described. I recommend that this paper be accepted with revisions, as the suggested changes can be addressed through additional experiments and clarifications.

**Rating:** 7 (Good paper, accept)

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
The paper presents a 3D object tracking method that consists of two primary modules: the Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The proposed approach demonstrates superior performance compared to previous state-of-the-art methods, which is a significant strength. The inclusion of a partial ablation study supports the effectiveness of the proposed components, and a thorough computational cost analysis enhances the method's practicality for real-world applications. Additionally, the innovative use of a point cloud generation task during training to improve feature expressiveness is noteworthy.

However, the paper is marred by several weaknesses. The writing quality is subpar, with numerous grammatical errors and unpolished sentences that detract from the overall clarity. Furthermore, the Template Feature Embedding Networks is overly complicated and relies heavily on engineering, yet it lacks a detailed ablation study to justify its design. The Voxel-to-BEV target localization network, while effective, appears to draw heavily from preceding works in 3D object detection, which raises concerns about its novelty. The paper also falls short in providing a thorough analysis of its limitations and potential societal impact. Lastly, the study of failure cases is limited, which is an important aspect of understanding the method's robustness.

To improve, the authors should thoroughly proofread the paper to correct grammatical errors and enhance clarity. A detailed ablation study for the Template Feature Embedding Networks is necessary to validate its complexity. The authors should also acknowledge and cite relevant prior works, such as PointPillar and SECOND, when discussing the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network. Additionally, a comprehensive analysis of the method's limitations and societal impact should be conducted. Expanding the study of failure cases would further strengthen the paper.

In conclusion, while the proposed 3D object tracking method outperforms existing approaches and has some interesting components, such as the use of point cloud generation during training, its overall quality is compromised by poor writing, concerns about novelty, and the lack of thorough analyses. With revisions addressing these issues, the paper could be significantly improved. Currently, it is marginally above the acceptance threshold, warranting a rating of 6.

The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The proposed siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications presents a well-designed algorithm that effectively addresses the challenging task of tracking objects in sparse point cloud inputs. The authors have demonstrated impressive performance on the KITTI benchmarks, surpassing existing solutions by a significant margin. The presentation of the paper is clear, with helpful visualizations that facilitate understanding.

One of the notable strengths of this work is its sensible algorithm design, which enables it to tackle a complex problem in autonomous driving. The experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are compelling, showcasing the approach's effectiveness in tracking objects from sparse point cloud inputs. Additionally, the authors' presentation is clear, making it easier to follow their methodology and appreciate the achievements.

However, there are areas that require attention. The approach exhibits worse performance on the cyclist category, suggesting limitations, potentially due to insufficient training data. This indicates that the method may not generalize well to categories with limited training data. Furthermore, the authors only provide visualizations in the supplementary material but do not include videos demonstrating tracking outputs. This omission makes it difficult to fully appreciate the tracking performance.

Some minor issues also need to be addressed. For instance, the color scheme in Fig. 3 could be improved for better clarity. Moreover, the authors should provide guidance on parameter tuning for new datasets, which is crucial for the generalizability of their approach to real autonomous driving scenarios. This guidance would help in understanding how the algorithm can be adapted to different environments and datasets.

To enhance the presentation and intuition about the tracking performance, I recommend including videos demonstrating tracking outputs. Providing such visual aids can significantly improve the readability and impact of the paper. Additionally, discussing potential extensions to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs could be beneficial, as it would help in understanding how the approach can be further developed for more comprehensive autonomous driving applications.

The authors have tested their approach only on the KITTI dataset, which might limit the generalizability of their findings. Moreover, parameter tuning in certain parts of the pipeline could affect performance, and guidance on this aspect would be valuable.

In conclusion, while the proposed algorithm shows significant improvement in tracking performance on sparse point cloud inputs, it has limitations, particularly in categories with insufficient training data. The authors are encouraged to provide code and consider additional RGB information for future research. Addressing the mentioned weaknesses and suggestions would strengthen the paper.

Given the significance of the contributions and the potential for improvement, I support the original decision: **6, Marginally above the acceptance threshold**. The authors have made a commendable effort in developing an effective object tracker for autonomous driving applications, and with revisions addressing the highlighted concerns, the paper could make an even more substantial impact in the field. 

**Recommendations for Revision:**

1. Include videos demonstrating tracking outputs.
2. Provide guidance on parameter tuning for new datasets.
3. Discuss potential extensions for dealing with RGB+LiDAR inputs.
4. Address minor issues such as the color scheme in Fig. 3.

By addressing these points, the authors can further enhance the clarity, generalizability, and impact of their work.

Discuss potential extensions to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs for autonomous driving applications.
main_points: The proposed algorithm shows significant improvement in tracking performance on sparse point cloud inputs.
The approach has limitations, particularly in categories with insufficient training data.
The authors are encouraged to provide code and consider additional RGB information for future research.
technical_details: The paper is tested only on the KITTI dataset.
Parameter tuning is required for certain places in the pipeline.

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?

Summary:
This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.

A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.

For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This review assesses the paper titled [Paper Title], which presents a method for single-object tracking from point clouds. The paper's primary contributions and shortcomings are discussed below.

The paper's method builds upon previous works, particularly P2B and SC3D, with a focus on dense detection rather than proposal-based methods. However, the novelty of the approach is not clearly distinguished from these predecessors. The authors claim that their shape-aware feature learning is a key innovation, but this seems similar to the scene completion regularization loss employed by SC3D.

One of the significant weaknesses of the paper is its presentation. The method section is overly detailed and lacks sufficient context, making it challenging to follow. The authors should consider rewriting this section to provide a clearer overview of their architecture design and its rationale.

Furthermore, the experimental results are limited. The paper would benefit from additional analyses, such as ablation studies and qualitative comparisons with state-of-the-art methods like SC3D and P2B. Specifically, it would be helpful to see how the proposed method performs in scenarios with sparse point clouds, as well as its limitations and potential negative societal impacts.

In terms of technical details, there is a discrepancy in the reported speed of P2B, which was mentioned to be 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, but the paper reports a significantly lower speed of 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU. This inconsistency should be clarified.

Despite these shortcomings, the paper shows some potential, particularly in its performance on objects with sparse points. However, to strengthen their argument, the authors should provide a more comprehensive evaluation of their method, including comparisons with SC3D and P2B, and discuss potential limitations.

Overall, I recommend that the authors revise their paper to address these concerns, provide a clearer presentation of their method, and include additional experimental results to support their claims. Based on the current submission, I would rate this paper as marginally above the acceptance threshold.

Rating: 6

Recommendation: Major revisions are required before this paper can be considered for acceptance. The authors should focus on clarifying their method, expanding their experimental evaluation, and addressing the limitations of their approach.

reviews.main_review:
The results  look promising.

My major concern if novelty is limited.  The proposed method is an ensemble of well-known components: template matching (eg. SiamRPN[1]), shape-completion and bev localization (e.g. LaserNet[2]).

Ablation studies of global and local template features are desired.

[1]Li, Bo, et al. "High performance visual tracking with siamese region proposal network." Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2018.
[2]Meyer, Gregory P., et al. "Lasernet: An efficient probabilistic 3d object detector for autonomous driving." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2019.

reviews.text:
This review assesses the proposed Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker, which combines template matching, shape-completion, and voxel-to-BEV target localization. The method shows promising results, but its novelty is a concern.

The proposed approach leverages well-known components, which is a significant drawback. The method's novelty is limited, as it primarily combines existing techniques, including template matching, shape-completion, and BEV localization. This lack of innovation is a substantial concern.

On the positive side, the method's results are promising, suggesting that it may be effective in its intended application. However, to strengthen the paper, I recommend that the authors conduct ablation studies on the global and local template features. This would help to clarify the contributions of each component and provide a better understanding of the method's performance.

Furthermore, the authors should provide a more detailed comparison to existing methods, such as SiamRPN and LaserNet. This would help to contextualize the proposed approach and demonstrate its advantages and disadvantages.

In conclusion, while the proposed method shows some promise, its lack of novelty and limited innovation are significant concerns. I recommend that the authors address these concerns by providing additional analysis and evaluation of their approach. Specifically, I recommend that they conduct ablation studies on the global and local template features and provide a more detailed comparison to existing methods. 

Based on the current submission, I would rate this paper as a 6, marginally above the acceptance threshold. With revisions addressing the above concerns, this paper has the potential to make a more significant impact.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method, demonstrating its performance on the KITTI dataset. While the approach shows promise, particularly with its intuitive design of local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction, there are significant areas that require clarification and improvement.

**Strengths:**

The authors have made notable efforts in designing and implementing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction, which intuitively improves performance. Additionally, the qualitative analysis of results for sparse point clouds provides a useful demonstration of the proposed method's effectiveness.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions:**

1. **Clarification of Main Contributions:** The paper's main contributions, particularly regarding shape completion and voxel-to-BEV target localization, require further clarification. The authors claim that shape completion in the search area is beneficial, but this needs more justification, especially considering prior work that has achieved comparable results without this component. A comparison with shape completion on the template, as done in prior work, would provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

2. **Network Design Justification:** The use of PointNet++, which is a four-year-old architecture, raises questions about the choice of network design. While PointNet++ has been widely used, more advanced methods have been proposed in recent years. The authors should justify their choice of network architecture, possibly through an ablation study that compares the performance of PointNet++ with more contemporary alternatives.

3. **Evaluation Protocol:** The evaluation protocol is limited, with testing performed only on two sequences of the KITTI dataset. This limited evaluation may not provide a robust or meaningful assessment of the method's performance. To strengthen their claims, the authors should conduct more comprehensive evaluations using additional datasets, such as nuScenes or Waymo, and provide a more detailed analysis of their results.

4. **Effectiveness of Voxel-to-BEV Target Localization:** The effectiveness of voxel-to-BEV target localization in segmenting z-axis center estimation needs clarification. An ablation study analyzing the impact of voxel-to-BEV target localization on the overall performance would be beneficial.

**Technical Details:**

The voxel size used is 0.3 meters in volumetric space, and the authors employ max-pooling on the z-axis to obtain dense BEV feature maps. These technical details are crucial for reproducing the results and understanding the method's performance.

**Conclusion:**

In conclusion, while the paper presents an interesting approach to 3D single-object tracking, it falls short in several areas, including the justification of main contributions, network design, and evaluation protocol. Addressing these weaknesses and providing more comprehensive evaluations and analyses would significantly strengthen the paper. Given the current state of the manuscript, I recommend that it is marginally above the acceptance threshold, warranting a rating of 6. However, I encourage the authors to revise and address the mentioned concerns to improve the paper's quality and contribution to the field.

**Rating:** 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D object tracking in point clouds, which, while interesting, falls short in several critical areas. Overall, I conclude that the paper is not good enough for publication in its current form, warranting a rating of 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection.

**Technical Overview**

The Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker utilizes a Siamese network to extract point features and adaptively learns the correlation between the template and search area. This approach generates a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud through shape-aware feature learning. The 3D center is decomposed into a 2D center and a z-axis center, which are regressed in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). While the technical details are well-described, the execution and evaluation are marred by significant limitations.

**Major Weaknesses**

A primary concern is the lack of clarity regarding the application focus of the proposed technique. The authors do not adequately specify whether their method is designed for single object or multiple object tracking, which is a crucial distinction. This ambiguity casts doubt on the framework's completeness, as it appears more akin to a 3D detection method than a comprehensive tracking framework. Essential components such as track initiation, termination, and occlusion handling are noticeably absent.

Furthermore, the related works and comparison frameworks are narrowly selected, omitting numerous relevant studies from both 2D and 3D multi-object tracking (MOT) communities. This selective review undermines the paper's credibility and contextual relevance. Additionally, the paper does not offer any novel theoretical contributions, functioning more as an application paper leveraging existing machine learning tools.

**Experimental Evaluation**

The experimental evaluation is limited to the KITTI dataset with a custom metric, rather than employing established benchmark metrics like MOTA or HOTA. Moreover, the approach is not evaluated on other 3D tracking datasets, such as NuScene, Waymo, or JRDB. This restricted evaluation raises concerns about the method's generalizability and robustness.

**Societal Impact and Limitations**

The authors also neglect to address potential limitations and societal impact, despite the significant implications of their work on applications like transportation and surveillance. This oversight is critical, as it leaves the paper lacking a comprehensive perspective on its contributions and consequences.

**Recommendations**

To strengthen the paper, I recommend the following:

1. **Clarify the application focus**: Provide clear details on whether the method is for single or multiple object tracking.
2. **Comprehensive review of related works**: Include a broader review of relevant works from both 2D and 3D MOT communities.
3. **Established benchmark metrics**: Use benchmark metrics like MOTA or HOTA for evaluation.
4. **Evaluation on diverse datasets**: Assess the approach on multiple 3D tracking datasets.

**Conclusion**

In conclusion, while the paper presents an interesting technical approach, its limitations in clarity, contribution, and experimental evaluation are substantial. Addressing the outlined weaknesses and implementing the suggested improvements could potentially elevate the paper to a publishable standard. However, in its current form, I support the decision of rating 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection.

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.

Main Review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents an analysis of the generalization error of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) in two distinct scenarios: (i) when the test task is one of the training tasks, and (ii) when the test task is unseen. The authors propose a new notion of stability and derive a generalization error bound based on total variation distance. This review assesses the strengths and weaknesses of the paper and provides recommendations for improvement.

**Strengths:**

The paper offers several significant contributions. Firstly, it presents an improved result over existing work by proposing a new notion of stability that effectively captures the dependence on the number of data points per task. This is a notable advancement, as it provides a more nuanced understanding of MAML's generalization capabilities. Additionally, the authors provide a tighter bound compared to existing work, which enhances the theoretical foundation of MAML. The approach of using total variation distance to analyze generalization bounds when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks is novel and merits further exploration.

**Weaknesses:**

One potential limitation of the paper is the assumption of a convex loss function, which may not hold in real-world applications of MAML. MAML is often utilized to learn deep neural networks, which typically involve non-convex losses. This assumption might restrict the applicability of the results to certain domains. Furthermore, the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML to 1 may not be realistic, as more complex tasks might require additional steps for adequate adaptation.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

To strengthen the paper, the authors could discuss the implications or challenges when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1. This would help to generalize their results and make them more applicable to a broader range of scenarios. Although the authors justify the weakness of assuming a convex loss function in Remark 2, further discussion on potential relaxations or alternative approaches could enhance the paper's robustness.

**Overall Assessment:**

The paper provides a thoughtful analysis of MAML's generalization error and proposes a new notion of stability and a generalization error bound based on total variation distance. While it has some limitations, particularly regarding the assumptions of convex loss functions and a single SGD step, the contributions of the paper are substantial. The suggestions provided are aimed at enhancing the applicability and generalizability of the results.

**Decision:**

Based on the assessment above, I recommend that the paper be accepted with a rating of 6, marginally above the acceptance threshold. The revisions suggested are expected to further improve the quality and applicability of the paper, making it a valuable addition to the field. 

The rating of 6 reflects that while the paper has some notable strengths and makes valuable contributions, it also has areas that require improvement to reach a higher level of excellence. With revisions addressing the identified weaknesses, the paper has the potential to make a more significant impact in the field of meta-learning. 

**Rating: 6**

reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.

Main Review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents an analysis of the generalization bounds for MAML-type algorithms, considering both homogeneous and heterogeneous cases. The authors provide a thorough examination of the generalization performance of these algorithms, offering valuable insights into their behavior.

**Strengths**

The paper's key strengths lie in its provision of generalization bounds in terms of data points for the homogeneous case, as well as its total variation-based analysis for the heterogeneous case. These contributions are significant, as they provide a deeper understanding of the algorithms' performance. Furthermore, the results are new and meaningful additions to the existing literature.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions**

However, the paper assumes strong convexity in the analysis of the recurring tasks case, which might be a limiting factor. Additionally, the algorithmic stability technique employed is mainly restricted to convex settings. To further strengthen the paper, the authors could elaborate on why non-convexity poses challenges in terms of stability notions. Moreover, discussing how the results would adapt to the bilevel case and including a discussion on the non-convex case in the paper would be beneficial.

**Main Contributions and Results**

The paper establishes that the generalization bound for MAML-type algorithms in the homogeneous case decreases at a rate of O(1/mn). In the heterogeneous case, the bound depends on the average total variation between training tasks and the unseen task. These findings are crucial, as they provide a better understanding of the algorithms' performance. The authors also highlight the importance of the number of samples per task in determining the generalization error.

**Technical Details**

The paper's technical aspects are well-explored, with a clear definition of (γ, K)-uniform stability and a thorough discussion of its connection to generalization error. The role of the number of samples per task in determining the generalization error is also well-explained.

**Conclusion and Recommendation**

In conclusion, the paper presents a thorough analysis of the generalization bounds for MAML-type algorithms. While there are some limitations, the contributions are significant, and the results are meaningful. I recommend that the paper be accepted, as it provides valuable insights into the behavior of these algorithms. The suggested improvements can be addressed through revisions, which would further strengthen the paper.

**Rating:** 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

This revised review maintains the same decision and provides a comprehensive evaluation of the paper, addressing all the key points and suggestions.

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
